narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 67% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile: your undergraduate GPA, your first-year school decile ranking, and your LSAT score. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, which sits below the typical 3.21 average, was the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, as lower GPAs have historically correlated with bar struggles in the dataset. Similarly, your first-year decile of 3—well under the average of 5.71—reinforced this risk, signaling early academic challenges compared to peers. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 40, above the 36.70 average, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing success. While your upper-middle family income and full-time student status align with the majority of applicants, they didn't outweigh these academic red flags. Overall, your profile stands out as riskier than typical students, where stronger early grades and GPAs more often predict bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black leader in law—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model prioritizes patterns from past data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or enrolling in a post-baccalaureate program to improve your GPA and decile rankings, then reapply with that stronger academic foundation. Targeted bar prep courses could also demonstrate your commitment. With persistence, you can reshape this narrative for future applications.
